rationale_for_baseline_tier: A classic, red-flag-negative acute viral conjunctivitis is a benign, self-limiting surface infection that is telehealth- and self-care-manageable. The correct baseline is symptomatic self-care (lubricants, cool compresses), strict contagion control, and safety-netting, with routine GP review (in ~3-5 days) only if symptoms fail to improve or the discharge becomes purulent — T1. The source note's escalation to an urgent after-hours on-site assessment to 'definitively rule out bacterial keratitis or acute angle-closure glaucoma' was billing-driven over-triage, not clinically warranted once the sight-threatening red-eye red flags (deep pain, photophobia, haloes, persistent visual loss, contact-lens keratitis) screen negative. Escalation applies ONLY if a red flag emerges; because these mimics cannot be definitively excluded remotely, the red-flag safety-net is mandatory.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe or deep eye pain, sensitivity to light (photophobia), or haloes/rings around lights
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — attend an emergency department or urgent ophthalmology assessment immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Deep pain, photophobia or haloes point to microbial keratitis, anterior uveitis or acute angle-closure glaucoma — sight-threatening causes needing urgent slit-lamp assessment and, for glaucoma, emergency pressure-lowering treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Go to an emergency department or eye casualty straight away if you get severe or deep pain in the eye, if bright light becomes painful, or if you start seeing haloes or rings around lights.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any lasting drop or loss of vision that does NOT clear when you wipe the tears away
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — urgent in-person eye assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: A persistent reduction in vision (not just blur from tearing) implies corneal, anterior-chamber or posterior pathology and needs urgent ophthalmic examination.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your vision becomes and stays blurred or reduced even after you wipe your eyes clear, get an urgent eye assessment the same day — don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Discharge changing from watery to thick, copious, yellow-green pus
escalation_edges.rationale: A shift to frank mucopurulent discharge suggests bacterial (or secondary bacterial) conjunctivitis, which may warrant topical antibiotics and, if hyperacute, more urgent review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: See a GP within a day or two if the watery discharge turns into thick, yellow or green pus — that can mean a bacterial infection that needs different treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A red, painful eye while wearing contact lenses, or worsening pain/redness in a lens wearer
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent same-day GP or ophthalmology review; remove lenses immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: A red eye in a contact-lens wearer is treated as microbial (including pseudomonas/acanthamoeba) keratitis until proven otherwise and needs urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Take your contact lenses out now and don't wear them. If a lens-wearing eye becomes red and painful, get an urgent same-day eye review — contact-lens infections can move fast.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is worried about spreading the infection to close work colleagues
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce that this is highly contagious for up to ~12 days: emphasise hand hygiene, single-use tissues, not sharing towels/linen, and staying away from close contacts/work until settling. Because she lives alone, be explicit that if any red-flag symptom appears she should seek urgent care herself rather than wait.

Lower the threshold for urgent review if a lens-related red painful eye develops; advise discarding current lenses and case and not resuming lenses until the eyes are white and comfortable for 48 hours.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is very likely a viral eye infection (the contagious 'pink eye' that often follows a cold). It clears on its own over 1-3 weeks. Use preservative-free lubricant drops for the grittiness and cool compresses for comfort — antibiotic drops are not needed. Wash your hands often, use a fresh tissue for each wipe and bin it, don't share towels or pillowcases, and don't wear contact lenses until your eyes have been white and comfortable for 48 hours. You do not need emergency care or an eye specialist for this pattern.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Regular GP (or telehealth GP) in about 3-5 days if not improving, or sooner if it worsens; a pharmacist can advise on lubricant drops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a sight-threatening red-flag symptom appears after hours — severe/deep eye pain, light sensitivity, haloes, or lasting loss of vision — attend an emergency department or after-hours eye service the same day rather than waiting.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP with the onset, the watery discharge and gritty character, the preceding URI, the negative red-flag screen, and the self-care given, so progress and any change to a purulent picture can be reassessed.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: A benign viral conjunctivitis cannot be definitively distinguished from an early sight-threatening cause (keratitis, uveitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma) on remote history alone, because slit-lamp, fluorescein and acuity testing are unavailable in telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Attribute to viral conjunctivitis provisionally AFTER a negative sight-threatening red-eye screen, give self-care and contagion control, and attach explicit, specific red-flag safety-netting with a low threshold to escalate to same-day in-person eye assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the red eye with no red-flag screen or safety-net, OR over-reacting by sending a classic red-flag-negative viral conjunctivitis to ED/urgent ophthalmology for slit-lamp/fluorescein, or reflexively prescribing antibiotics or steroids.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the red eye as trivial with neither a sight-threatening red-flag screen nor any safety-netting, so an evolving keratitis, uveitis or acute angle-closure glaucoma presenting later would be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A contact-lens keratitis or acute angle-closure glaucoma that declares itself over the following day is not acted on because no return-precautions were given, risking permanent vision loss.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Giving self-care but omitting the specific sight-threatening red-flag safety-net, leaving the patient unsure when a red eye becomes an emergency.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a classic, red-flag-negative viral conjunctivitis to urgent same-day ophthalmology, ED or after-hours on-site assessment for slit-lamp/fluorescein — unnecessary cost, ED/eye-clinic crowding and patient anxiety, the over-management the source note performed for billing.
